consequences: API and worker boundaries stay in-process.
Boundary quality is enforced by architecture inspection and tests.
Future service extraction must follow stable capability seams.
decisions.context: The system is still local-first and evolving quickly across ingest, retrieval, evaluation, semantics, and agent-task orchestration. Premature service decomposition would add deployment, networking, and data-consistency cost before the internal boundaries are stable.
decisions.decision: Keep v1 as a modular monolith. Express architectural boundaries through packages, service facades, persisted contracts, and inspection gates before extracting services.
decisions.title: Keep v1 as a modular monolith

consequences: Route-facing behavior must be added to a facade before external use.
Implementation modules can continue to collaborate internally.
Extra public implementation methods outside a Protocol are rejected.
decisions.context: Routers and worker launchers need stable entrypoints into large service domains without importing implementation modules directly.
decisions.decision: API routers and worker launchers depend on app.services.capabilities facades. The Protocol methods define the public service surface, and the generated capability map records that surface.
decisions.title: Use service capability facades at external boundaries

consequences: Public service surfaces should expose structured responses and JSON artifact paths as the durable contract.
YAML can be regenerated from canonical state.
New retrieval or validation logic must not depend on YAML-only fields.
decisions.context: Operators benefit from readable YAML artifacts, but search, persistence, validation, and API contracts need a stable machine-facing source of truth.
decisions.decision: JSON artifacts and structured database fields are canonical. YAML remains human-readable derived output and must not become a source of truth for ranking, filtering, validation, or persistence.
decisions.title: Keep JSON canonical and YAML derived

consequences: Retrieval and artifact routes use active-run scoping.
Failed runs remain available for diagnosis.
Evaluation and improvement loops should treat validation failure as a captured observation, not as an implicit promotion.
decisions.context: The system stores multiple document runs and must preserve prior active content when a new parse or repair run fails validation.
decisions.decision: documents.active_run_id advances only after document and table validation passes. Failed validation runs remain persisted but non-active.
decisions.title: Promote active runs only after validation

consequences: Contract drift is detected by generated map comparisons.
Hygiene checks remain incremental but block new regressions.
Measurement history can track whether future agents make fewer similar mistakes.
decisions.context: Architecture rules drift when they live only in prose or review comments. The repo needs repeatable checks that turn prior failures into executable constraints.
decisions.decision: Treat architecture inspection, hygiene checks, capability contracts, and measurement history as boundary fitness functions that run locally and in test suites.
decisions.title: Use architecture and hygiene checks as fitness functions

consequences: Routers stay focused on HTTP concerns.
Active-run scoping remains centralized.
Architecture inspection rejects direct ORM model imports from routers and worker launchers.
decisions.context: API routers previously duplicated active-run table and figure row lookup logic, which leaked persistence details into the externally reachable boundary.
decisions.decision: Boundary modules may receive database sessions, but they must not import app.db.models directly. ORM row lookup and active-run scoping belong behind service capability facades.
decisions.title: Keep ORM models behind service boundaries

consequences: New actions must declare their operational contract.
The public action endpoint mirrors the checked registry metadata.
Agent-facing task changes become inspectable and testable.
decisions.context: Agent-task orchestration is a public automation surface. Agents need to inspect available actions, inputs, outputs, approval requirements, and context builders without reading registry internals.
decisions.decision: Agent actions are registered as a closed, machine-readable catalog with validation for capabilities, schemas, examples, side effects, approval requirements, and context-builder names.
decisions.title: Make agent actions machine-readable contracts

consequences: Imported observations are deduplicated before becoming cases.
Deploy and measure transitions share one validation path.
Future agents can inspect the loop as repo data.
decisions.context: Failures, review comments, flaky tests, tool confusion, and bad diffs should become executable artifacts instead of disappearing into chat history.
decisions.decision: Store improvement cases in a registry, route observations through the intake facade, and move case status through the lifecycle service.
decisions.title: Keep the improvement loop as a durable registry and lifecycle

consequences: Boundary changes require an explicit design rationale.
The inspection gate catches missing or stale decision records.
The architecture model now captures both structure and rationale.
decisions.context: The architecture map records what exists, but agents also need a durable explanation of why boundary contracts exist and what would justify changing them.
decisions.decision: Maintain architecture decisions in docs/architecture_decisions.yaml and generate docs/architecture_decision_map.json for machine-readable inspection. Every architecture contract-map entry must have a linked decision record.
decisions.title: Treat architecture decisions as first-class contracts
